name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى جَاعِلٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ ٱلدِّمَآءَ وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٠)
```

```english
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’ (30)
```

```arabic
وَعَلَّمَ ءَادَمَ ٱلْأَسْمَآءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ عَلَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ فَقَالَ أَنۢبِـُٔونِى بِأَسْمَآءِ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’ (31)
```

```arabic
قَالُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَلِيمُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣٢)
```

```english
They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.’ (32)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰٓـَٔادَمُ أَنۢبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَنۢبَأَهُم بِأَسْمَآئِهِمْ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’ (33)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses inform us of the purpose behind sending man down to this world, the reality of establishing the vicegerency (الخلافة) — *the status of being a representative or deputy of Allah* — upon the earth, and what its effects and properties are. Unlike other prophetic stories, this account does not occur in the Quran except in this single location.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ** **When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’** [Qarai: 2:30]. Discussion regarding the meaning of "speech" (القول) from the Exalted, as well as speech from the angels and Satan, will follow, Allah willing.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالُوا أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ** (They said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood...) up to His saying: **When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’** [Qarai: 2:30]. This suggests that they understood the occurrence of corruption and bloodshed from His saying, the Glorified: **إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** **When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’** [Qarai: 2:30]. This is because an earthly being, insofar as it is material (مادي), is composed of the powers of anger (الغضبية) and desire (الشهوية). Furthermore, this realm is a realm of mutual interference (التزاحم), limited in its dimensions, and abundant in frictions. Its compositions are prone to dissolution, and its orders and rectifications are subject to corruption and the locus of nullification. Life cannot be sustained therein except through collective life (الحيوة النوعية), and survival cannot be perfected except through social gathering (الاجتماع) and cooperation (التعاون). Thus, it cannot be free from corruption and the shedding of blood.

From this, they understood that the intended vicegerency (الخلافة) would not occur on earth except through a multitude of individuals and a social system among them that ultimately leads to corruption and bloodshed. Vicegerency—which is the standing of one thing in the place of another—cannot be realized unless the vicegerent (الخليفة) reflects the one who appointed him (المستخلف) in all his existential affairs, effects, rulings, and management, insofar as he is the one being represented. Allah, the Glorified, in His existence, is named by the Most Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and described by the Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا), including attributes of Beauty (الجمال) and Majesty (الجلال). He is transcendent (منزه) in His essence from deficiency and sanctified (مقدس) in His action from evil and corruption—exalted is His greatness. An earthly vicegerent, being as he is, is not fit for this representation, nor does his existence—tainted by every deficiency and blemish—reflect the Divine Existence, which is sanctified and transcendent from all deficiencies and non-existences (الأعدام). Where is the dust compared to the Lord of lords?

This speech from the angels was in the context of seeking to know what they were ignorant of and clarifying what was problematic for them regarding the matter of this vicegerent; it was not an act of objection or contention at all. The proof for this is their saying, as narrated by Allah the Exalted: **إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** **They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.’** [Qarai: 2:32], where they prefaced the sentence with the causal "indeed" (إنّ), indicating their submission to what follows it—so understand this.

In summary, their argument returns to the idea that the establishment of vicegerency is only so that the vicegerent may reflect his Appointer through his glorification (التسبيح) with His praise and his sanctification (التقديس) of Him through his existence. However, the earthly nature does not allow him to do so; rather, it drags him toward corruption and evil. The goal of this appointment—which is glorification and sanctification in the sense of reflection mentioned—is already achieved through our glorification with Your praise and our sanctification of You. Thus, we are Your vicegerents, or make us Your vicegerents. What, then, is the benefit of establishing this earthly vicegerency for You? Allah, the Glorified, refuted this by His saying: **إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** **When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth,’ they said, ‘Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood, while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity?’ He said, ‘Indeed I know what you do not know.’** [Qarai: 2:30] and **وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا** **And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’** [Qarai: 2:31].

**This context suggests:**

**First:** That the mentioned vicegerency was specifically the vicegerency of Allah the Exalted. It was not the vicegerency of a species of earthly beings who were on the earth before man and became extinct, whom Allah then intended to replace with man, as some commentators have conjectured. This is because the answer Allah gave them—teaching Adam the Names—would not be appropriate for that. Accordingly, the vicegerency is not restricted to the person of Adam (peace be upon him); rather, his children share it with him without exclusivity. The meaning of teaching the Names would then be the depositing of this knowledge (العلم) within man such that its effects appear from him gradually and perpetually; if he were guided to the path, he could bring it from potentiality (القوة) into actuality (الفعل). The universality of the vicegerency is supported by His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ جَعَلَكُمْ خُلَفَاءَ مِن بَعْدِ قَوْمِ نُوحٍ** **Do you consider it odd that there should come to you a reminder from your Lord through a man from among yourselves, so that he may warn you? Remember when He made you successors after the people of Noah, and increased you vastly in creation. So remember Allah’s bounties so that you may be felicitous.’** [Qarai: 7:69], and His saying: **ثُمَّ جَعَلْنَاكُمْ خَلَائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ** **Then We made you successors on the earth after them that We may observe how you will act.** [Qarai: 10:14], and His saying: **وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَاءَ الْأَرْضِ** **Is He who answers the call of the distressed [person] when he invokes Him and removes his distress, and makes you successors on the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the admonition that you take.** [Qarai: 27:62].

**Second:** That He, the Glorified, did not deny corruption and bloodshed regarding the earthly vicegerent, nor did He belie the angels in their claim of glorification and sanctification; rather, He affirmed them in what they claimed. Instead, He manifested something else: that there is a matter which the angels are unable to carry and cannot endure, while this earthly vicegerent can endure it. For he reflects a matter from Allah the Exalted and carries a secret from Him that is not within the capacity of the angels. Inevitably, this matter compensates for the corruption and bloodshed.

Allah the Exalted later replaced His saying: **إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** (I know what you do not know) with His saying: **أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكُمْ إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ غَيْبَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** **He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’** [Qarai: 2:33]. What is meant by this "Unseen" (الغيب) is the Names themselves, not Adam's knowledge of them. The angels did not know that there were Names they did not know; it was not that they knew of the existence of such Names but were ignorant that Adam knew them. Otherwise, there would have been no reason for Allah to ask them about the Names—which is obvious. Rather, the appropriate course would have been to limit the matter to His saying: **يَا آدَمُ أَنبِئْهُم بِأَسْمَائِهِمْ** **He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’** [Qarai: 2:33] so it would become clear to them that Adam knew them, rather than asking the angels about them. This context indicates that they claimed the vicegerency and were convinced of its absence in Adam, whereas it was necessary for the vicegerent to know the Names. So He asked them about the Names and they were ignorant of them, while Adam knew them. Thus, his fitness for it and their lack thereof was established. Allah concluded the question with His saying: **إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ** **And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’** [Qarai: 2:31], which suggests they had claimed something whose necessary prerequisite was knowledge of the Names.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا ثُمَّ عَرَضَهُمْ** **And He taught Adam the Names, all of them; then presented them to the angels and said, ‘Tell me the names of these, if you are truthful.’** [Qarai: 2:31] suggests that these Names—or rather their named objects (المسميات)—were living, rational beings (موجودات أحياء عقلاء), veiled behind the curtain of the Unseen (الغيب). It also suggests that the knowledge of their names was not like the kind of knowledge we have of the names of things. Otherwise, by Adam informing them of the names, the angels would have become knowledgeable and become like Adam, equal to him. There would have been no honor (اكرام) or distinction (كرامة) for Adam in that Allah taught him names but did not teach them. Had He taught them, they would have been like Adam or more noble than him, and there would have been nothing in that to convince them or invalidate their argument.

What argument is established by Allah teaching a man the science of language (علم اللغة) and then boasting of him and establishing the proof against honored angels—who do not precede Him in speech and act by His command—by saying, "This is My vicegerent and capable of My honor, rather than you"? And by saying, "Inform Me of the languages that humans will later devise among themselves for communication if you are truthful in your claim or your question regarding My vicegerency"? Furthermore, the perfection of language lies in the knowledge of the intentions of the heart, and the angels have no need for speech in that regard; they receive intentions without mediation. Thus, they possess a perfection above the perfection of speech. In short, the knowledge the angels gained through Adam informing them of the names is different from the reality of the knowledge of the Names (حقيقة العلم بالأسماء) that Adam gained through Allah's teaching. One of the two matters was possible for the angels and within their capacity, while the other was not. Adam only deserved the Divine vicegerency through the knowledge of the Names, not the informing of them, for the angels said in their response: **سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا** **They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.’** [Qarai: 2:32], thereby negating knowledge.

It has become clear from what has passed that the knowledge of the names of these named objects must be such that it uncovers their realities (حقائق) and the essences of their existences (أعيان وجوداتهم), rather than merely what linguistic convention (الوضع اللغوي) provides in terms of giving a concept (المفهوم). These known named objects are external realities (حقائق خارجية) and concrete existences (وجودات عينية) which are, nonetheless, hidden under the veil of the Unseen—the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. Knowledge of them as they truly are was, firstly, possible for an earthly being but not a heavenly angel, and secondly, instrumental in the Divine vicegerency.

**The "Names" (الأسماء) in His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا** (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them) is a plural defined by the definite article (ال), which signifies universality (العموم) as scholars have stated. Additionally, it is emphasized by His saying: **كُلَّهَا** (all of them). Thus, what is meant is every name that applies to a named object, without restriction or specific reference. Then, His saying: **عَرَضَهُمْ** (He presented them) indicates that every name—that is, its named object—possesses life and knowledge, and is yet under the veil of the Unseen, the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. While the annexation (إضافة) of "Unseen" to "the heavens and the earth" could in some contexts be an annexation of "from" (من), signifying partitive meaning, the context here—which is the manifestation of the perfection of His power and encompassment, and the incapacity and deficiency of the angels—requires that the annexation be that of "belonging to" (اللام). This signifies that the Names are matters hidden from the heavenly and earthly world, outside the perimeter of the cosmos (الكون).

When you reflect on these aspects—namely the universality of the Names, the fact that their named objects possess life and knowledge, and their being the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—you will necessarily conclude that they correspond to what is alluded to in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ وَمَا نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَّعْلُومٍ** **There is not a thing but that its sources are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure.** [Qarai: 15:21]. There, the Glorified informs us that everything to which the name "thing" applies has with Him stored treasuries (خزائن) that are enduring with Him, neither exhausted nor measured by a limit, nor restricted by a boundary. Measure (القدر) and boundary (الحد) only occur at the level of sending down (الإنزال) and creation (الخلق). The multiplicity within these treasuries is not of the nature of numerical multiplicity (الكثرة العددية) that is inseparable from measurement and limitation, but rather a multiplicity of ranks (المراتب) and degrees (الدرجات). Some discussion on this will follow in Surah al-Hijr, Allah willing.

In conclusion, those whom Allah presented to the angels are exalted beings (موجودات عالية) preserved with Allah, veiled by the veils of the Unseen. Allah sent down every name in the world through their goodness and blessing, and everything in the heavens and the earth derived from their light and splendor. Despite their multiplicity and number, they do not multiply like the multiplication of individuals, nor do they differ like the difference of persons; rather, the matter there revolves around ranks and degrees. The descent of a name from them is of this type of descent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ** **He said, ‘O Adam, inform them of their names,’ and when he had informed them of their names, He said, ‘Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth, and that I know whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal?’** [Qarai: 2:33]. These two categories were part of the relative Unseen (الغيب النسبي) which constitutes part of the heavens and the earth. For this reason, it was contrasted with His saying: "I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth," so as to include both types of the Unseen: that which is outside the earthly and heavenly world and that which is not.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ** (you used to conceal). The restriction of concealment with the past continuous "used to" (كنتم) suggests that there was something concealed specifically regarding Adam and the establishment of his vicegerency. This can be inferred from His saying in the following verse: **فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ** **And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the faithless.** [Qarai: 2:34].

It appears that Iblis was a disbeliever (كافراً) before that time, and that his refusal to prostrate was connected to that; he had harbored this within himself. It also appears that the prostration of the angels and Iblis's refusal occurred between His saying: "I know what you do not know" and His saying: "I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal." This also reveals the secret behind replacing His saying: "I know what you do not know" the second time with His saying: "I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "How did the angels know to say: **أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَن يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ** (Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood), unless they had already seen someone who causes corruption and sheds blood?"

**I say:** This may refer to a cycle on earth prior to this cycle of the children of Adam, as mentioned in the reports. This does not contradict what was previously stated—that the angels understood this from His saying: **إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** (I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth). In fact, the report is not complete without that; otherwise, this statement would have been a blameworthy analogy (قياس) by the angels, like the analogy of Iblis.

Also in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from him (peace be upon him), Zurarah said: "I visited Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and he asked: 'What do you have of the traditions of the Shi'a?' I said: 'I have many of them, and I have even considered lighting a fire to burn them.' He (peace be upon him) said: 'Hide them and you will forget what you find objectionable in them.' Then the thought of human beings occurred to me, and he said: 'How did the angels know when they said: "Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood"?'" He said: "Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) used to say when he narrated this tradition: 'This is a refutation against the Qadariyyah (القدرية) — *those who deny predestination and attribute absolute creative power to human will*.'" Then Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "Adam (peace be upon him) had a close friend among the angels in heaven. When Adam was sent down from heaven to earth, the angel felt lonely and complained to Allah, asking Him for permission [to visit him]. He permitted him, so he descended to him and found him sitting in a desolate part of the earth. When Adam saw him, he placed his hand on his head and let out a loud cry." Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "They narrate that he made all of creation hear it." The angel said to him: "O Adam, I see only that you have disobeyed your Lord and burdened yourself with what you cannot bear. Do you know what Allah said to us regarding you and what we replied to Him?" He said: "No." He said: "He said: 'I am indeed going to set a viceroy on the earth.' We said: 'Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?' Thus, He created you to be on the earth; is it fitting for you to be in heaven?" Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "By Allah, he consoled Adam with this three times."

**I say:** It is inferred from the narration that Adam's garden was in heaven, and other narrations regarding this will follow.

Also in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu al-Abbas, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: **وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا** (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them). What did He teach him?" He said: "The earths, the mountains, the mountain passes, and the valleys." Then he looked at a carpet beneath him and said: "And this carpet is among what He taught him."

In the same *Tafsir*, from al-Fudayl ibn al-Abbas, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: **وَعَلَّمَ آدَمَ الْأَسْمَاءَ كُلَّهَا** (And He taught Adam the Names, all of them). What are they?" He said: "The names of the valleys, the plants, the trees, and the mountains of the earth."

In the same *Tafsir*, from Dawud ibn Sarhan al-Attar, who said: "I was with Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) when he called for the dining table and we ate. Then he called for the basin (الطست) and the hand-towel (الدست سنان). I said: 'May I be your ransom! Is His saying: "And He taught Adam the Names, all of them"—are the basin and the hand-towel part of it?' He (peace be upon him) said: 'The mountain paths and the valleys,' and he gestured with his hand thus and so."

In *al-Ma'ani*, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, taught Adam the names of all His Proofs (حججه) — *the divinely appointed guides who serve as the evidence of Allah's truth to creation*. Then He presented them—while they were spirits (أرواح)—to the angels and said: 'Inform Me of the names of these if you are truthful in your claim that you are more entitled to the vicegerency on earth due to your glorification and sanctification than Adam.' They said: 'Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us; indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise.' Allah, the Blessed and Exalted, said: 'O Adam, inform them of their names.' When he informed them of their names, they realized their magnificent status with Allah, exalted be His mention. They knew that they were more entitled to be the vicegerents of Allah on His earth and His Proofs over His creation. Then He veiled them from their sight and required their servitude through their guardianship (ولايتهم) and love for them. He said to them: 'Did I not tell you that I know the Unseen of the heavens and the earth and I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal?'"

**I say:** By referring back to the preceding commentary, you will understand the meaning of these narrations and that there is no contradiction between them and what preceded. It has already been stated that His saying: **وَإِن مِّن شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِندَنَا خَزَائِنُهُ** (There is not a thing but that its primary sources are with Us) implies that there is nothing except that it has an existence in the treasuries of the Unseen, and that these things before us only came into existence by descending from there. Every name placed in correspondence to one of these named objects is a name for what is in the treasuries of the Unseen. Thus, whether it is said that Allah taught Adam the things that are in the treasuries of His Unseen—which are the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—or it is said that He taught Adam the names of everything—which are the Unseen of the heavens and the earth—the intended meaning and the result are one, and this is clear.

Appropriate to this context are several reports regarding the "clay" (الطينة) — *the primordial substance from which beings are created*, such as what is narrated in *al-Bihar* from Jabir ibn Abdillah, who said: "I said to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم: 'What was the first thing Allah created?' He said: 'The light of your Prophet, O Jabir. Allah created it, then He created from it every good. Then He stationed it before Him in the station of proximity (مقام القرب) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into parts: He created the Throne (العرش) from one part, the Pedestal (الكرسي) from one part, and the bearers of the Throne and the inhabitants of the Pedestal from one part. He stationed the fourth part in the station of love (مقام الحب) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into parts: He created the Pen (القلم) from one part, the Tablet (اللوح) from one part, and Paradise (الجنة) from one part. He stationed the fourth part in the station of fear (مقام الخوف) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into portions: He created the angels from one portion, the sun from one portion, and the moon from one portion. He stationed the fourth part in the station of hope (مقام الرجاء) for as long as Allah willed. Then He divided it into portions: He created the intellect (العقل) from one portion, knowledge (العلم) and forbearance (الحلم) from one portion, and protection from sin (العصمة) and success (التوفيق) from one portion. He stationed the fourth part in the station of modesty (مقام الحياء) for as long as Allah willed. Then He looked upon it with the eye of awe, and that light perspired, and one hundred and twenty-four thousand drops dripped from it. From every drop, Allah created the spirit of a prophet and a messenger. Then the spirits of the prophets exhaled, and from their breaths, Allah created the spirits of the friends of Allah (الأولياء), the martyrs (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين).'"

**I say:** The reports regarding these meanings are numerous and mutually supporting (متظافرة). If you cast a glance of reflection and scrutiny upon them, you will find them to be witnesses to what we have presented. A portion of the discussion regarding some of them will follow. Beware of dismissing such noble traditions—transmitted from the mines of knowledge and the springs of wisdom—as being among the fabrications of the Sufis or their delusions. For creation has secrets. Here are the scientists from the various classes of mankind, sparing no effort in researching the secrets of nature since humanity began to spread; yet whenever a single known fact appears to them, many unknowns become manifest to them. This is the world of nature, the narrowest and lowest of worlds; so what do you think of what lies beyond it, which are the worlds of light and vastness?
